perturbation.original_text: 1.1 For the purposes of this Agreement, Confidential Information means any data or proprietary information of the Discloser that is not generally known to the public or has not yet been revealed, whether in tangible or intangible form, whenever and however disclosed, including, but not limited to: (i) any scientific or technical information, invention, d e s i g n , p r o c e s s , procedure, formula, improvement, technology or method; (ii) any concepts, samples, reports, data, know-how, works-in-progress, designs, drawings, photographs, development tools, s p e c i f i c a t i o n s , software programs, source code, object code, flow charts, and databases; (iii) any marketing strategies, plans, financial information, or projections, operations, sales estimates, business plans and performance results relating European IPR Helpdesk | Page 4 of 8 to the Discloser’s past, present or future business activities, or those of its affiliates, subsidiaries and affiliated companies; (iv) trade secrets; plans for products or services, and customer or supplier lists; (v) any other information that should reasonably be recognized as Confidential Information by the Discloser.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 For the purposes of this Agreement, Confidential Information, hereinafter referred to as 'Protected Data', means any data or proprietary information of the Discloser that is not generally known to the public or has not yet been revealed, whether in tangible or intangible form, whenever and however disclosed, including, but not limited to: (i) any scientific or technical information, invention, d e s i g n , p r o c e s s , procedure, formula, improvement, technology or method; (ii) any concepts, samples, reports, data, know-how, works-in-progress, designs, drawings, photographs, development tools, s p e c i f i c a t i o n s , software programs, source code, object code, flow charts, and databases; (iii) any marketing strategies, plans, financial information, or projections, operations, sales estimates, business plans and performance results relating European IPR Helpdesk | Page 4 of 8 to the Discloser’s past, present or future business activities, or those of its affiliates, subsidiaries and affiliated companies; (iv) trade secrets; plans for products or services, and customer or supplier lists; (v) any other information that should reasonably be recognized as Confidential Information by the Discloser.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the definition of 'Confidential Information' to include 'Protected Data' without consistently using 'Protected Data' throughout the document. This creates confusion because it is never reffered again as 'Protected Data', and continues being 'Confidential Information'. This introduces a contradiction, because it does not clearly states in the definition of terms if 'Confidential Information' and 'Protected Data' are being reffered as the same, and does not use this new term consistently.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.2 The Recipient will not disclose and will keep confidential the information received, except to its employees, representatives or agents who need to have access to the Confidential Information for the purpose of carrying out their duties in connection with the permitted purposes specified in clause 2. The Recipient will inform them about the confidential quality of the information provided and will ensure that their agreement is obtained to keep it confidential on the same terms as set forth in this Agreement. Hence the Recipient will be responsible for ensuring that the obligations of confidentiality and non-use contained herein will be strictly observed and will assume full liability for the acts or omissions made for its personnel representatives or agents.
perturbation.justification: The changed text redefines 'Confidential Information' as 'Protected Data' but the contradicted text refers only to 'Confidential Information'. This introduces a misalignment because the term 'Protected Data' is not consistently used, creating a contradiction within the document as it's unclear if these terms are interchangeable or distinct, creating uncertainty, following the 'Misaligned Terminology' definition.

perturbation.original_text: 1.3 Confidential Information shall be identified either by marking it, in the case of written materials, or, in the case of information that is disclosed orally or written materials that are not marked, by notifying the Recipient of the confidential nature of the information. Such notification shall be done orally, by e-mail or written correspondence, or via other appropriate means of communication.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.3 Confidential Information, also known as 'Private Details,' shall be identified either by marking it, in the case of written materials, or, in the case of information that is disclosed orally or written materials that are not marked, by notifying the Recipient of the confidential nature of the information. Such notification shall be done orally, by e-mail or written correspondence, or via other appropriate means of communication.
perturbation.explanation: Introducing 'Private Details' as an alternative term for 'Confidential Information' but never using 'Private Details' again. This creates uncertainty about whether all mentions of 'Confidential Information' also imply 'Private Details', leading to possible disputes over what is covered by the agreement. Since it's not used again, but it's implied to be reffering to the same as 'Confidential Information', it might affect different parts of the text where this contradiction can be highlighted.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.3 The Recipient will use the Confidential Information exclusively for the permitted purpose stated in clause 2 and not use the information for its own purposes or benefit.
perturbation.justification: The change introduces 'Private Details' as an alternative term for 'Confidential Information' but never uses 'Private Details' again. This creates uncertainty about whether all mentions of 'Confidential Information' also imply 'Private Details'. Clause 3.3 states "The Recipient will use the Confidential Information exclusively for the permitted purpose". The added term introduces ambiguity, as it is not clear if the obligations in 3.3 also apply to 'Private Details', potentially contradicting the intended scope of the clause. This fits the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' where multiple terms are used interchangeably without clear definition, leading to conflicting obligations.

perturbation.original_text: European IPR Helpdesk | Page 5 of 8 terms of this Agreement or reference or access to any Confidential Information pertaining to the Discloser.
perturbation.changed_text: European IPR Helpdesk | Page 5 of 8 terms of this Agreement or reference or access to any Sensitive Information pertaining to the Discloser.
perturbation.explanation: Substituting 'Confidential Information' with 'Sensitive Information' creates ambiguity. While seemingly similar, 'Sensitive Information' can have different legal interpretations, particularly in data protection contexts. The contract does not define 'Sensitive Information,' leaving it open to interpretation and potentially weakening the protection afforded to the Discloser's information. If it's not defined previously if 'Confidential Information' is the same as 'Sensitive Information' it creates a contradiction, since it's not clear if the document refers to both the same, or different definitions.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.5 The Recipient shall treat all Confidential Information with the same degree of care as it accords to its own Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Confidential Information' to 'Sensitive Information' introduces ambiguity because 'Sensitive Information' is undefined in the contract, while 'Confidential Information' is referenced elsewhere (3.5) regarding the standard of care for its treatment. Since the definition of 'Sensitive Information' is missing, it is unclear if the recipient should treat both the same. This creates a contradiction because the contract lacks clarity on whether the same degree of care applies to 'Sensitive Information' as to 'Confidential Information,' potentially weakening the protection intended for the Discloser's information. The perturbation type is Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 2. Purpose of the Disclosure of Confidential Information The Discloser and Recipient will enter on discussions regarding future collaboration toward European Funded Project in the field of [….] or [will submit a proposal for a collaborative project in response to the call (identify the call) under (identify the EU funded Programme)] or [will enter into or evaluate alternatives for partnership or collaboration with [name of the Recipient] for the purpose of [identify the undertaking intended to perform or achieve].
perturbation.changed_text: 2. Purpose of the Disclosure of Confidential Details The Discloser and Recipient will enter on discussions regarding future collaboration toward European Funded Project in the field of [….] or [will submit a proposal for a collaborative project in response to the call (identify the call) under (identify the EU funded Programme)] or [will enter into or evaluate alternatives for partnership or collaboration with [name of the Recipient] for the purpose of [identify the undertaking intended to perform or achieve].
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Confidential Information' to 'Confidential Details' in the section title but not consistently throughout the text introduces a contradiction. This raises questions about whether the purpose of disclosure only applies to 'Confidential Details' and not other forms of 'Confidential Information,' potentially limiting the scope of protection. Since it's the title of the Section, it might be understood that every other mentioned of 'Confidential Information' reffers exclusively to 'Confidential Details', but is not confirmed, and in the text it's assumed that both are equal.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.6 All Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement shall be and remain under the property of the Discloser and nothing contained in this Agreement shall be construed as granting or conferring any rights to such Confidential Information on the Recipient. Principally, nothing in this Agreement shall be deemed to grant to the Recipient a licence expressly or by implication under any patent, copyright or other intellectual property right. The Recipient hereby acknowledges and confirms that all European IPR Helpdesk | Page 6 of 8 the existing and future intellectual property rights related to the Confidential Information are exclusive titles of the Discloser. For the sake of clarity based in good faith, the Recipient will not apply for or obtain any intellectual property protection in respect of the Confidential Information received. Likewise any modifications and improvements thereof by the Recipient shall be the sole property of the Discloser.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Confidential Information' to 'Confidential Details' in the section title introduces ambiguity regarding the scope of the agreement. While previously all Confidential Information was protected, the change suggests that only 'Confidential Details' are covered by Section 2. This directly contradicts Section 3.6, which states that 'All Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement shall be and remain under the property of the Discloser'. If 'Confidential Information' is now interpreted more narrowly, the protections afforded by Section 3.6 might not apply to all types of confidential information, creating a clear contradiction and potential unenforceability. The 'Misaligned Terminology' creates this contradiction as defined.

perturbation.original_text: 3.7 The Recipient shall promptly return or destroy all copies (in whatever form reproduced or stored), including all notes and derivatives of the Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement, upon the earlier of (i) the completion or termination of the dealings contemplated in this Agreement; (ii) or the termination of this Agreement; (iii) or at the time the Discloser may request it to the Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.7 The Recipient shall promptly return or destroy all copies (in whatever form reproduced or stored), including all notes and derivatives of the Disclosed Data disclosed under this Agreement, upon the earlier of (i) the completion or termination of the dealings contemplated in this Agreement; (ii) or the termination of this Agreement; (iii) or at the time the Discloser may request it to the Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'Confidential Information' with 'Disclosed Data' only in this clause creates ambiguity. Is 'Disclosed Data' a subset of 'Confidential Information,' or does it encompass all information disclosed, regardless of its confidential nature? This could lead to disputes about which information must be returned or destroyed. Since the rest of the text is reffering to 'Confidential Information', if only this clause mentions 'Disclosed Data', it is not clear what it reffers to, and if 'Confidential Information' should be also considered 'Disclosed Data' and what are the differences, if any.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.8 Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Recipient may retain such of its documents as required to comply with mandatory law, provided that such Confidentiality Information or copies thereof shall be subject to an indefinite c o n f i d e n t i a l i t y obligation.
perturbation.justification: The change replaces 'Confidential Information' with 'Disclosed Data' in clause 3.7, while clause 3.8 refers to 'Confidentiality Information'. This creates a misalignment in terminology, as it's unclear whether 'Disclosed Data' is equivalent to 'Confidentiality Information' or a distinct subset. This ambiguity directly impacts the obligation in 3.8 regarding retaining documents under indefinite confidentiality, as it's uncertain if this obligation extends to 'Disclosed Data' if it's not considered 'Confidential Information', causing a strong in-text contradiction as per the provided Misaligned Terminology definition.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1.1 This Agreement shall remain in effect for a term of [number of months or years] term. Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Recipient’s duty to hold in confidence Confidential Information that was disclosed during the term shall remain in effect indefinitely, save otherwise agreed.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1.1 This Agreement shall remain in effect for a term of [number of months or years] term. Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Recipient’s duty to hold in confidence Secret Information that was disclosed during the term shall remain in effect indefinitely, save otherwise agreed.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Confidential Information' to 'Secret Information' in the duration clause, without any prior definition, creates uncertainty about which information the Recipient has a duty to protect after the agreement terminates. This could lead to disputes about the scope of the confidentiality obligation. Since there's no 'Secret Information' defined in the document, it is not known if there's a difference between 'Confidential Information' and 'Secret Information', also implying that the rest of the text might have an issue due to the same term changing without a proper definition of terms.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.10 The Recipient agrees that the Discloser will suffer irreparable damage if its Confidential Information is made public, released to a third party, or otherwise disclosed in breach of this Agreement and that the Discloser shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief against a threatened breach or continuation of any such a breach and, in the event of such breach, an award of actual and exemplary damages from any court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Confidential Information' to 'Secret Information' in the Changed Text introduces a contradiction because the Contradicted Text references 'Confidential Information' and establishes specific rights and remedies related to its disclosure. The change creates ambiguity and uncertainty about which information is protected after the agreement terminates. This contradiction aligns with the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction', where the contract uses multiple terms interchangeably without defining them, leading to conflicting obligations. Specifically, it is unclear if the obligations related to 'Confidential Information' extend to 'Secret Information', potentially rendering the contradicted clause unenforceable with respect to 'Secret Information'.
